figures.caption: Figure 2: Adjusted per-biome FID for MD4 (patch 2) and MD4 (patch 4) and DDPM (patch 2) models. Lower is better.

Figure 16: Block-conditioned 5 × 5 × 1 worlds generated by our MD4 (patch 2) model. Each pair shows the context block pattern (left) and the corresponding generated world (right). Block patterns are automatically sliced to 323 chunks and used as block context for inpainting, in addition to overlapping blocks from neighboring cells.

Figure 17: Block-conditioned inpainting using the MD4 patch 4 model. We generate 4 variants for each context pattern, and show the best looking generation for each. In cases where all variants were of high quality, we select the one most resembling the patch 2 version shown in figure 6
Table 7: Estimated biome percentages and final biome distribution for the natural occurrence dataset.
Figure 19: Example of the user study interface. Each candidate set is rendered in 3D using the in-game Minecraft textures, and can be rotated independently to view chunks from all angles.
Figure 3: Adjusted per-biome FID comparison between MD4 (patch 4) models across three dataset composition variants. Lower is better.
Table 11: Per-biome non-air Jensen–Shannon divergence between generated and real block-name distributions. Lower is better; zero indicates an exact match.
Table 10: Per-biome non-air vocabulary coverage for each model. Coverage is the fraction of block types present in real chunks that also appear in generated samples. Higher is better.
Figure 9: Randomly selected biome-conditioned samples generated by our MD4 (patch 2) model across all 15 natural biome classes.
Figure 10: Randomly selected biome-conditioned samples generated by our DDPM (patch 2) model across all 15 natural biome classes.
Figure 22: Train and validation loss curves for DDPM patch 2 model
Figure 20: Train and validation loss curves for MD4 patch 2 model
Figure 21: Train and validation loss curves for MD4 patch 4 model

Table 5: Per-biome FID comparison between MD4 models trained with human data vs. without. Lower is better. Bold indicates the lowest available FID for each biome. A dash indicates the model has no value for that biome.
Figure 8: 5× 5× 1 worlds generated by the MD4 model trained on the human-augmented dataset. Each world uses one of the six human-authored maps as biome condition for all cells
Figure 12: Randomly selected biome-conditioned samples generated by our MD4 (patch 4) model, trained on the natural occurrence dataset, across all 15 natural biome classes
Figure 13: Randomly selected biome-conditioned samples generated by our MD4 (patch 4) model, trained on the village boosted dataset, across all 15 natural biome classes
Figure 11: Randomly selected biome-conditioned samples generated by our MD4 (patch 4) model, trained on the balanced dataset, across all 15 natural biome classes
Figure 4: Biome-conditioned samples generated by our MD4 and DDPM models across 5 natural biome classes
Figure 5: Inpainting from an existing chunk’s blocks. (A) Inpainting using the source chunk’s biome during sampling. (B) Inpainting using Ice, Extreme Hills, and Beaches biome during sampling.

Table 15: Per-biome normalized preference rates (wins / appearances within each biome). Bold indicates the highest source in each biome. Total n gives the total number of source appearances in that biome across all source match-ups.
Table 8: Per-biome FID scores for MD4 (patch 2), sampled using 1000, 100, and 10 sampling steps. Lower is better. Bold indicates the lowest FID for each biome.
Figure 18: Standard inpainting from a 8× 8× 32 chunk and from human-authored block contexts using our DDPM (patch 2) model.
Figure 15: Three 5× 5× 1 worlds generated by the three dataset variant MD4 (patch 4) models. Top row: balanced dataset. Middle: Natural occurrence dataset. Bottom: Village boosted dataset.
Figure 14: Six unconditional 5× 5× 1 worlds generated by our MD4 (patch 2) model

Figure 6: Inpainting using manually authored block patterns as context. Each pair shows the context blocks (left) and the corresponding inpainted chunk (right). Invisible air blocks are placed above all manually authored blocks, to ensure the model does not bury the authored pattern underground.

Figure 7: Large-scale “worlds” generated via outpainting. Left: A 5× 5× 1 world generated without any conditioning. Center: A 4× 4× 1 world generated with biome conditioning. Right: 4× 4× 1 world generating using manually authored block contexts, specifying a ring of water and a volcano.

Table 12: Preference-FID model by biome on model-vs-model trials. Agreement is the proportion of trials where which the participant selected the model with the lower per-biome FID. Confidence intervals are Wilson 95% intervals.
Table 6: Collapsed mapping from Minecraft-native labels to final biome labels used in DREAMCUBED.
